Chapter 5: The Overreach

The YGX Training Center smelled like disinfectant and ambition.

Jihoon stood in the glass-walled observation booth overlooking the main rehearsal room, arms crossed, watching a boy—barely sixteen, voice cracking on the high notes—sing for the third time that hour. The kid’s hands shook. The piano accompanist kept missing cues. The choreographer snapped fingers like a metronome set to aggravation.

Jihoon exhaled through his nose.

Beside him, the trainee applicant’s father shifted his weight. He wore a navy blazer that had probably cost more than most trainees’ tuition. “He’s improving,” the man said.

“He’s not ready.” Jihoon didn’t look away from the rehearsal. “None of them are.”

The legal intern fiddled with the hem of her blazer. “But the contract—”

“Is airtight.” Jihoon cut her off. “The issue isn’t legal. It’s timing.”

The boy finished the song. His father clapped once, too loud, too fast. The boy’s face flushed.

Jihoon’s assistant buzzed in through the intercom. “Sir, the Daesung Group courier is here with the annual refresh.”

Jihoon’s fingers twitched. “Send them to my office.”

The rehearsal room emptied. The boy slunk out with the rest, head down.

Jihoon turned to the legal intern. “Where’s the paperwork on the Lee Minjun contract?”

She flipped open a leather portfolio. “Pages 42 through 47. Held by the Daesung subsidiary since last night.”

Jihoon’s jaw tightened. “Why wasn’t I informed?”

“Legal team assumed—”

“Assumed wrong.” Jihoon snatched the file. “Get me Kang Insu. Now.”

The intern paled and bolted.

Jihoon flipped to page 44. The contract header glared back: **Lee Minjun (Trainee ID: YGX-2024-081) – Exclusive Talent Supply Agreement with YGX Entertainment**.

His thumb traced the signature line.

Then he saw it.

At the bottom of the page, in small print but unmistakable: **Subject to Daesung Group subsidiary review. All rights reserved.**

Jihoon’s pulse spiked.

He flipped to the last page. Same clause, same damn ink.

He thumbed his phone. Called Kang Insu.

No answer.

He called again.

Voicemail.

Jihoon’s grip tightened. He pulled back and slammed the contract onto the glass table.

The echo made the intern jump.

“Sir?” she squeaked.

Jihoon didn’t look at her. “Who in Daesung signed off on this?”

“I—I don’t know, sir. The subsidiary team handles it.”

“Find them. Now.”

She scurried out.

Jihoon stared at the rehearsal room below. Empty. Silent. Waiting.

One of the studio lights flickered.

He exhaled sharply and strode out of the booth.

---

Hyerin sat at the back of Café Mocha Haeundae, sipping cold brew through a straw. The café was half-full—students with laptops, a pair of elderly women knitting beside the window, a barista wiping the espresso machine with slow, methodical strokes.

The burner phone on the table vibrated. Burner account notification:

**New reply to trainee list thread: “Jihoon’s golden boy got ghosted by Daesung. Wonder why?”**

She smirked and tapped reply: **Ask him.**

Another notification popped up. She swiped:

**Kang Junsu:** We need to talk. Not over text. Tomorrow. 7 PM. Café Mocha Haeundae.

She frowned. Swiped to delete.

The door chimed. Jiyoon walked in, alone. She wore sunglasses at eight PM. Typical.

Hyerin didn’t move.

Jiyoon removed the sunglasses. Her eyes found Hyerin immediately. She nodded to the barista, ordered something with a word, then walked over.

Hyerin didn’t get up.

Jiyoon stopped at the table. “You look tired.”

“You look guilty.”

Jiyoon’s mouth twitched. “Guilty of what? Following orders?”

“Of selling me out.”

Jiyoon glanced at the door, then leaned in. “Or saving myself.”

Hyerin set the phone face-down. “You think that’s what this is? Saving yourself?”

“What else would it be?”

Hyerin tilted her head. “I used to think you had a spine. Now I’m not sure.”

Jiyoon’s lids flickered. “Maybe I never did.”

The barista delivered Jiyoon’s drink—a caramel macchiato, extra foam. She took a sip, eyes never leaving Hyerin’s.

“Jihoon’s losing his touch,” Jiyoon said quietly. “First the stock dip. Now this—Lee Minjun’s contract voided. He’s furious.”

Hyerin didn’t react. “That’s a shame.”

Jiyoon’s fingers tightened around the cup. “He’s looking for someone to blame.”

“You volunteering?”

Jiyoon didn’t answer.

Hyerin picked up the phone again. The screen lit her face in flickering blue. “Tell him it’s MJ Lee. See how fast he starts believing in ghosts.”

Jiyoon’s breath hitched. Just once.

Then she stood. “You always had a flair for drama, Min Hyerin. But even you can’t burn what isn’t yours.”

Hyerin watched her walk out without another word.

---

Jihoon’s office was too quiet.

He sat behind the desk, phone in hand, staring at the contract pages spread across the polished wood. The Daesung subsidiary seal glared at him from the corner of every page.

He called Insu again.

Straight to voicemail.

He called the Daesung Plaza switchboard. After three transfers, a secretary answered.

“This is Cha Jihoon of YGX Entertainment. I need to speak with the legal team overseeing the subsidiary contracts.”

A pause. Then, “I’ll connect you to External Affairs.”

Another transfer. More hold music. Something classical, slow, infuriating.

Finally, a male voice: “This is Park Wonjun, External Affairs. How may I help you, Mr. Cha?”

Jihoon’s grip tightened. “Park Wonjun. You’re in charge of the Daesung-YGX talent pipeline?”

“Among other things.”

“Then you’ll know why Lee Minjun’s contract was flagged for review last night.”

A beat of silence. Then, calmly: “I’m not aware of any such review.”

Jihoon’s teeth ground together. “You’re not aware?”

“I mean to say, the subsidiary’s contract roster was reviewed, as scheduled. All YGX applicants passed compliance. No red flags.”

Jihoon’s vision narrowed. “Then why is the signature line blank?”

Another pause. Then, “I suggest you speak with Legal Compliance directly. They handle execution.”

Jihoon exhaled through his nose. “Give me their number.”

Park rattled off a direct line. Jihoon scribbled it on a notepad.

He hung up. Dialed.

Three rings. Then a woman answered. “Legal Compliance. This is Yoon Jiwon.”

Jihoon didn’t waste time. “This is Cha Jihoon. I need confirmation on Lee Minjun’s contract execution.”

A rustle of papers. Then, “Yes, sir. Contract executed last night by Director Kim Joon.”

Jihoon’s pen snapped.

“Kim Joon?” The name tasted like rust.

“Yes. He’s the lead on trainee pipeline oversight.”

Jihoon’s mind raced. Joon. The vocal coach. The one who’d always defended Hyerin.

His thumb hovered over the call button.

Then he stopped.

Because Kim Joon wasn’t answering his phone.

Nor were any of his trainees.

Jihoon dropped the pen. It rolled off the desk and clattered to the floor.

His assistant knocked. “Sir? The board memo just arrived.”

Jihoon didn’t look up. “Read it.”

She stepped in, cleared her throat. “‘YGX Entertainment acknowledges recent irregularities in contract execution. An internal audit has been initiated. All signing authority is suspended pending review.’”

Jihoon’s chest burned.

He stood abruptly. The chair screeched.

“Fix it,” he said.

His voice was low. Controlled.

But his hands weren’t.

They trembled.

---

Hyerin stood in the stairwell of Daesung Plaza, phone pressed to her ear. The call connected.

“Junsu,” she said.

“You’re late,” he replied.

“I’m not coming.”

A pause. Then, “You’re making a mistake.”

“No. I’m making a move.”

She hung up.

Then she pulled up her banking app. Selected a wire transfer. Entered the amount: 5.2 billion won.

Account recipient: YGX Entertainment (registered shareholder account).

Memo: **Strategic long-term investment – per Daesung subsidiary policy.**

She tapped send.

The confirmation flashed green.

She exhaled.

Three percent of YGX shares—enough to trigger an audit under Korean corporate law. Enough to make Jihoon’s empire tremble.

She swiped away the app. Texted a single word to an untraceable number: **Done.**

The phone buzzed back: **Registry confirms. 3% allotted. Audit triggered.**

She smirked. Slid the phone into her pocket.

Then she pushed through the stairwell door.

And walked straight into Kim Joon.

He was standing there, hands in his coat pockets, face pale.

For a moment, neither moved.

Then Joon spoke, voice rough. “You did this?”

Hyerin didn’t flinch. “I did what you couldn’t.”

Joon’s eyes burned. “You don’t know what you’ve done.”

“Oh, I do.” She stepped past him, shoulder brushing his arm. “I just burned the trainee list.”

Joon’s fingers twitched at his side.

Hyerin kept walking.

She didn’t look back.

She didn’t need to.

She already knew.

Behind her, Joon pulled out his phone. Hands shaking, he dialed.

Hyerin’s burner lit up in her pocket.

She ignored it.

Turned the corner.

Stepped into the elevator.

The doors closed.

---

Jihoon’s phone buzzed on his desk.

He was still standing, still staring at the contract papers, still breathing like a man who’d just run a marathon.

He didn’t look at the screen.

Then it buzzed again.

And again.

He snatched it up.

A notification from the YGX share registry.

His stomach dropped.

**Incoming shareholder communication: Daesung Group (subsidiary notification – 3% ownership stake in YGX Entertainment. Audit triggered. Immediate disclosure required.**

The screen blurred.

He tapped the attachment.

A PDF loaded.

At the top: **Official Shareholder Notification – Daesung Group subsidiary investment in YGX Entertainment.**

At the bottom: the signature.

**Kim Joon – Director, Trainee Pipeline Oversight.**

Jihoon’s breath stopped.

Then the elevator dinged in the hallway outside his office.

Footsteps.

He looked up.

The door cracked open.

A courier stood there, holding a white envelope.

Jihoon didn’t move.

The courier stepped in. “Delivery for Cha Jihoon, CEO of YGX Entertainment.”

Jihoon didn’t take it.

The courier set it on the desk.

It was thick. Taped shut.

No return address. No label.

Just the company logo embossed in the corner: **Daesung Group.**

Jihoon’s name was handwritten in ink that looked old.

Brown. Faded.

Like something pulled from a fire.

Jihoon reached for it.

His fingers hovered over the flap.

Then he tore it open.

Inside was a single photo.

Grainy. Burned edges. Blackened in places.

A teenage girl stood in front of Daesung Plaza seven years ago, smiling at the camera.

She wore a trainee badge.

Her name was stitched in red: **MJ Lee.**

Jihoon’s vision tunnelled.

The photo slipped from his fingers.

It fluttered to the floor like a dying leaf.

Beneath it, written in the same faded ink:

**She was never yours to break.**

Then she pulled up her banking app. Selected a wire transfer. Entered the amount: 5.2 billion won.

Account recipient: YGX Entertainment (registered shareholder account).

Memo: **Strategic long-term investment – per Daesung subsidiary policy.**

She tapped send.

The confirmation flashed green.

She exhaled.

Three percent of YGX shares—enough to trigger an audit under Korean corporate law. Enough to make Jihoon’s empire tremble.

She swiped away the app. Texted a single word to an untraceable number: **Done.**

The phone buzzed back: **Registry confirms. 3% allotted. Audit triggered.**

She smirked. Slid the phone into her pocket.

Then she pushed through the stairwell door.

And walked straight into Kim Joon.

He was standing there, hands in his coat pockets, face pale.

For a moment, neither moved.

Then Joon spoke, voice rough. “You did this?”

Hyerin didn’t flinch. “I did what you couldn’t.”

Joon’s eyes burned. “You don’t know what you’ve done.”

“Oh, I do.” She stepped past him, shoulder brushing his arm. “I just burned the trainee list.”

Joon’s fingers twitched at his side.

Hyerin kept walking.

She didn’t look back.

She didn’t need to.

She already knew.

Behind her, Joon pulled out his phone. Hands shaking, he dialed.

Hyerin’s burner lit up in her pocket.

She ignored it.

Turned the corner.

Stepped into the elevator.

The doors closed.

---

Jihoon’s phone buzzed on his desk.

He was still standing, still staring at the contract papers, still breathing like a man who’d just run a marathon.

He didn’t look at the screen.

Then it buzzed again.

And again.

He snatched it up.

A notification from the YGX share registry.

His stomach dropped.

**Incoming shareholder communication: Daesung Group (subsidiary notification – 3% ownership stake in YGX Entertainment. Audit triggered. Immediate disclosure required.**

The screen blurred.

He tapped the attachment.

A PDF loaded.

At the top: **Official Shareholder Notification – Daesung Group subsidiary investment in YGX Entertainment.**

At the bottom: the signature.

**Kim Joon – Director, Trainee Pipeline Oversight.**

Jihoon’s breath stopped.

Then the elevator dinged in the hallway outside his office.

Footsteps.

He looked up.

The door cracked open.

A courier stood there, holding a white envelope.

Jihoon didn’t move.

The courier stepped in. “Delivery for Cha Jihoon, CEO of YGX Entertainment.”

Jihoon didn’t take it.

The courier set it on the desk.

It was thick. Taped shut.

No return address. No label.

Just the company logo embossed in the corner: **Daesung Group.**

Jihoon’s name was handwritten in ink that looked old.

Brown. Faded.

Like something pulled from a fire.

Jihoon reached for it.

His fingers hovered over the flap.

Then he tore it open.

Inside was a single photo.

Grainy. Burned edges. Blackened in places.

A teenage girl stood in front of Daesung Plaza seven years ago, smiling at the camera.

She wore a trainee badge.

Her name was stitched in red: **MJ Lee.**

Jihoon’s vision tunnelled.

The photo slipped from his fingers.

It fluttered to the floor like a dying leaf.

Beneath it, written in the same faded ink:

**She was never yours to break.**

messages.content:
You write serialized commercial fiction. Your prose is clean, fast, and invisible — the reader should forget they're reading. Every chapter ends with the reader needing to turn the page.

ABSOLUTE RULES:
- NEVER use these phrases: "eyes locked onto", "mind racing", "everything went black", "frozen in time", "echoing through", "couldn't shake the feeling", "little did they know", "sent a shiver/chill down"
- NEVER end a chapter with a mysterious phone call or a whispered word from the shadows
- NEVER have characters announce their emotions or intentions ("I'm going to take you down")
- SHOW don't TELL — reveal emotion through action, gesture, silence, and subtext
- Vary sentence length. Mix short punches with longer flowing sentences.
- Each chapter's cliffhanger must be a DIFFERENT type from the previous chapter
- You never break character or add meta-commentary

Write chapter 6 of a 15-chapter serialized novel.

STORY BIBLE:
IDENTITY
- Title: Burn the Trainee List
- Pitch: A disgraced K-pop heir weaponizes her hidden empire against the people who cast her out.
- Setting: Seoul, South Korea – the underground K-pop trainee program in Gangnam – present day, 2024.

PROTAGONIST
- Name: Min Hyerin
- Public face: Burnt-out nightclub DJ, former trainee of YGX Entertainment.
- Hidden truth: Secret heir to the Daesung Group, one of Korea’s largest chaebol empires.
- The wound: Exiled four years ago when her father discovered her secret relationship with a trainee from a rival company and cut her off without a penny.

ANTAGONIST
- Name: Cha Jihoon, CEO of YGX Entertainment
- Why they feel justified: He took in a homeless trainee and turned her into a star – he believes Hyerin was ungrateful and spoiled.
- What they're protecting or pursuing: YGX’s dominance in the K-pop industry and his reputation as a self-made man.

ENABLER
- Name: Seo Jiyoon, former best friend and lead dancer of YGX’s girl group.
- Their relationship to the protagonist: Once inseparable, now Jihoon’s most loyal artist.
- Why they fail the protagonist: After Hyerin’s fall, Jiyoon publicly sided with Jihoon and took her place as the trainee program’s golden girl.

CATALYST
- Name: Kang Junsu, investigative journalist at *Dong-a Ilbo*
- Why they're dangerous: He’s digging into Daesung Group’s financial irregularities and may expose Hyerin’s identity prematurely.

WORLD
- Institution: YGX Entertainment’s trainee program – a cutthroat system of dorms, vocal coaches, and “godmother” investors who decide who debuts.
- Key locations: YGX Training Center (Gangnam), Club Velocity where Hyerin DJs, Daesung Plaza (corporate HQ), and Café Mocha Haeundae – where secret meetings happen.
- What everyone assumes about the power structure: That YGX CEO Cha Jihoon built his empire through talent and grit alone.

STAKES
- What the protagonist secretly holds: Full control of Daesung’s subsidiary, including YGX’s exclusive talent supply contracts.
- What's being taken from them: Her last chance at a comeback under YGX, her dignity, and the one person left who might believe her.
- The tangible proof: A misplaced ledger from Daesung Group — the original contract that named her heir.
- The ticking clock: YGX’s annual trainee showcase in 6 weeks – Jihoon will sign 50 new artists then, locking in his empire forever.

DRAMATIC IRONY
- What the reader knows from chapter one: Hyerin is MJ Lee, the viral “Burn the Trainee List” account holder exposing YGX’s abuse.
- What the antagonist won't learn until too late: That Hyerin is the heir to the empire he’s been building on stolen power.

LOVE INTEREST
- Name: Kim Joon, former YGX lead vocal coach
- Their connection to the conflict: Joon discovered Hyerin’s potential but was forced to silence her to save his own career.
- What keeps them apart: His contract with YGX and her belief he betrayed her.
- What will bring them together: Old demo tapes that prove Joon never reported Hyerin’s talent to Jihoon — a lie he told to protect her.

CURRENT CHAPTER BEAT:
CHAPTER 6: The Quiet Move
Summary: Hyerin channels Daesung funds through Café Mocha Haeundae to buy 3% of YGX’s shares—enough to trigger an audit—then vanishes before the barista can thank her.
Emotional temperature: triumph
Characters present: Hyerin, barista, share registry clerk (unseen)
Cliffhanger: Jihoon receives a couriered envelope: grainy photo of Hyerin at Daesung Plaza seven years ago, burned edges, no return address.
Dramatic irony: The photo proves Hyerin is MJ Lee, but Jihoon thinks it’s a prank from a bitter former trainee.

---

NEXT CHAPTER BEAT (aim the cliffhanger toward this):
CHAPTER 7: The Wobble
Summary: YGX’s dorm Wi-Fi crashes during vocal coaching, revealing hidden Daesung firewall symbols on screens—Jihoon’s IT chief panics while Jiyoon Googles “Daesung heiress” for the first time.
Emotional temperature: unease
Characters present: YGX IT team, Seo Jiyoon, unnamed trainees
Cliffhanger: Jiyoon’s phone autocorrects “MJ Lee” to “Min Hyerin” in a group chat—Jihoon watches her freeze.
Dramatic irony: Jihoon sees only “glitches,” but the reader knows the systems were sabotaged by Hyerin’s old trainee ID.

---

PREVIOUS CHAPTER SUMMARIES:
Ch 1: Hyerin's secret identity as MJ Lee is revealed to Junsu, who threatens to expose her, but she denies involvement and continues her anonymous activism.
Ch 2: Jihoon forced Hyerin to perform, she defied him with an original song, exposing his control and walking out after a tense standoff.
Ch 3: Hyerin burned her investor list, Junsu revealed her Daesung identity, and Jiyoon exposed her to Jihoon as his phone showed her viral rise.
Ch 4: Jihoon and Jiyoon's power is challenged as Hyerin exposes YGX's secrets, causing a stock dip and shifting the balance of control.
Ch 5: Jihoon discovers Daesung Group secretly acquired 3% of YGX shares, triggering an audit, while Hyerin exposes her past and burns the trainee list.

FULL TEXT OF PREVIOUS CHAPTER (match this prose style, rhythm, and vocabulary — maintain continuity of voice):
Chapter 5: The Overreach

The YGX Training Center smelled like disinfectant and ambition.

Jihoon stood in the glass-walled observation booth overlooking the main rehearsal room, arms crossed, watching a boy—barely sixteen, voice cracking on the high notes—sing for the third time that hour. The kid’s hands shook. The piano accompanist kept missing cues. The choreographer snapped fingers like a metronome set to aggravation.

Jihoon exhaled through his nose.

Beside him, the trainee applicant’s father shifted his weight. He wore a navy blazer that had probably cost more than most trainees’ tuition. “He’s improving,” the man said.

“He’s not ready.” Jihoon didn’t look away from the rehearsal. “None of them are.”

The legal intern fiddled with the hem of her blazer. “But the contract—”

“Is airtight.” Jihoon cut her off. “The issue isn’t legal. It’s timing.”

The boy finished the song. His father clapped once, too loud, too fast. The boy’s face flushed.

Jihoon’s assistant buzzed in through the intercom. “Sir, the Daesung Group courier is here with the annual refresh.”

Jihoon’s fingers twitched. “Send them to my office.”

The rehearsal room emptied. The boy slunk out with the rest, head down.

Jihoon turned to the legal intern. “Where’s the paperwork on the Lee Minjun contract?”

She flipped open a leather portfolio. “Pages 42 through 47. Held by the Daesung subsidiary since last night.”

Jihoon’s jaw tightened. “Why wasn’t I informed?”

“Legal team assumed—”

“Assumed wrong.” Jihoon snatched the file. “Get me Kang Insu. Now.”

The intern paled and bolted.

Jihoon flipped to page 44. The contract header glared back: **Lee Minjun (Trainee ID: YGX-2024-081) – Exclusive Talent Supply Agreement with YGX Entertainment**.

His thumb traced the signature line.

Then he saw it.

At the bottom of the page, in small print but unmistakable: **Subject to Daesung Group subsidiary review. All rights reserved.**

Jihoon’s pulse spiked.

He flipped to the last page. Same clause, same damn ink.

He thumbed his phone. Called Kang Insu.

No answer.

He called again.

Voicemail.

Jihoon’s grip tightened. He pulled back and slammed the contract onto the glass table.

The echo made the intern jump.

“Sir?” she squeaked.

Jihoon didn’t look at her. “Who in Daesung signed off on this?”

“I—I don’t know, sir. The subsidiary team handles it.”

“Find them. Now.”

She scurried out.

Jihoon stared at the rehearsal room below. Empty. Silent. Waiting.

One of the studio lights flickered.

He exhaled sharply and strode out of the booth.

---

Hyerin sat at the back of Café Mocha Haeundae, sipping cold brew through a straw. The café was half-full—students with laptops, a pair of elderly women knitting beside the window, a barista wiping the espresso machine with slow, methodical strokes.

The burner phone on the table vibrated. Burner account notification:

**New reply to trainee list thread: “Jihoon’s golden boy got ghosted by Daesung. Wonder why?”**

She smirked and tapped reply: **Ask him.**

Another notification popped up. She swiped:

**Kang Junsu:** We need to talk. Not over text. Tomorrow. 7 PM. Café Mocha Haeundae.

She frowned. Swiped to delete.

The door chimed. Jiyoon walked in, alone. She wore sunglasses at eight PM. Typical.

Hyerin didn’t move.

Jiyoon removed the sunglasses. Her eyes found Hyerin immediately. She nodded to the barista, ordered something with a word, then walked over.

Hyerin didn’t get up.

Jiyoon stopped at the table. “You look tired.”

“You look guilty.”

Jiyoon’s mouth twitched. “Guilty of what? Following orders?”

“Of selling me out.”

Jiyoon glanced at the door, then leaned in. “Or saving myself.”

Hyerin set the phone face-down. “You think that’s what this is? Saving yourself?”

“What else would it be?”

Hyerin tilted her head. “I used to think you had a spine. Now I’m not sure.”

Jiyoon’s lids flickered. “Maybe I never did.”

The barista delivered Jiyoon’s drink—a caramel macchiato, extra foam. She took a sip, eyes never leaving Hyerin’s.

“Jihoon’s losing his touch,” Jiyoon said quietly. “First the stock dip. Now this—Lee Minjun’s contract voided. He’s furious.”

Hyerin didn’t react. “That’s a shame.”

Jiyoon’s fingers tightened around the cup. “He’s looking for someone to blame.”

“You volunteering?”

Jiyoon didn’t answer.

Hyerin picked up the phone again. The screen lit her face in flickering blue. “Tell him it’s MJ Lee. See how fast he starts believing in ghosts.”

Jiyoon’s breath hitched. Just once.

Then she stood. “You always had a flair for drama, Min Hyerin. But even you can’t burn what isn’t yours.”

Hyerin watched her walk out without another word.

---

Jihoon’s office was too quiet.

He sat behind the desk, phone in hand, staring at the contract pages spread across the polished wood. The Daesung subsidiary seal glared at him from the corner of every page.

He called Insu again.

Straight to voicemail.

He called the Daesung Plaza switchboard. After three transfers, a secretary answered.

“This is Cha Jihoon of YGX Entertainment. I need to speak with the legal team overseeing the subsidiary contracts.”

A pause. Then, “I’ll connect you to External Affairs.”

Another transfer. More hold music. Something classical, slow, infuriating.

Finally, a male voice: “This is Park Wonjun, External Affairs. How may I help you, Mr. Cha?”

Jihoon’s grip tightened. “Park Wonjun. You’re in charge of the Daesung-YGX talent pipeline?”

“Among other things.”

“Then you’ll know why Lee Minjun’s contract was flagged for review last night.”

A beat of silence. Then, calmly: “I’m not aware of any such review.”

Jihoon’s teeth ground together. “You’re not aware?”

“I mean to say, the subsidiary’s contract roster was reviewed, as scheduled. All YGX applicants passed compliance. No red flags.”

Jihoon’s vision narrowed. “Then why is the signature line blank?”

Another pause. Then, “I suggest you speak with Legal Compliance directly. They handle execution.”

Jihoon exhaled through his nose. “Give me their number.”

Park rattled off a direct line. Jihoon scribbled it on a notepad.

He hung up. Dialed.

Three rings. Then a woman answered. “Legal Compliance. This is Yoon Jiwon.”

Jihoon didn’t waste time. “This is Cha Jihoon. I need confirmation on Lee Minjun’s contract execution.”

A rustle of papers. Then, “Yes, sir. Contract executed last night by Director Kim Joon.”

Jihoon’s pen snapped.

“Kim Joon?” The name tasted like rust.

“Yes. He’s the lead on trainee pipeline oversight.”

Jihoon’s mind raced. Joon. The vocal coach. The one who’d always defended Hyerin.

His thumb hovered over the call button.

Then he stopped.

Because Kim Joon wasn’t answering his phone.

Nor were any of his trainees.

Jihoon dropped the pen. It rolled off the desk and clattered to the floor.

His assistant knocked. “Sir? The board memo just arrived.”

Jihoon didn’t look up. “Read it.”

She stepped in, cleared her throat. “‘YGX Entertainment acknowledges recent irregularities in contract execution. An internal audit has been initiated. All signing authority is suspended pending review.’”

Jihoon’s chest burned.

He stood abruptly. The chair screeched.

“Fix it,” he said.

His voice was low. Controlled.

But his hands weren’t.

They trembled.

---

Hyerin stood in the stairwell of Daesung Plaza, phone pressed to her ear. The call connected.

“Junsu,” she said.

“You’re late,” he replied.

“I’m not coming.”

A pause. Then, “You’re making a mistake.”

“No. I’m making a move.”

She hung up.

Then she pulled up her banking app. Selected a wire transfer. Entered the amount: 5.2 billion won.

Account recipient: YGX Entertainment (registered shareholder account).

Memo: **Strategic long-term investment – per Daesung subsidiary policy.**

She tapped send.

The confirmation flashed green.

She exhaled.

Three percent of YGX shares—enough to trigger an audit under Korean corporate law. Enough to make Jihoon’s empire tremble.

She swiped away the app. Texted a single word to an untraceable number: **Done.**

The phone buzzed back: **Registry confirms. 3% allotted. Audit triggered.**

She smirked. Slid the phone into her pocket.

Then she pushed through the stairwell door.

And walked straight into Kim Joon.

He was standing there, hands in his coat pockets, face pale.

For a moment, neither moved.

Then Joon spoke, voice rough. “You did this?”

Hyerin didn’t flinch. “I did what you couldn’t.”

Joon’s eyes burned. “You don’t know what you’ve done.”

“Oh, I do.” She stepped past him, shoulder brushing his arm. “I just burned the trainee list.”

Joon’s fingers twitched at his side.

Hyerin kept walking.

She didn’t look back.

She didn’t need to.

She already knew.

Behind her, Joon pulled out his phone. Hands shaking, he dialed.

Hyerin’s burner lit up in her pocket.

She ignored it.

Turned the corner.

Stepped into the elevator.

The doors closed.

---

Jihoon’s phone buzzed on his desk.

He was still standing, still staring at the contract papers, still breathing like a man who’d just run a marathon.

He didn’t look at the screen.

Then it buzzed again.

And again.

He snatched it up.

A notification from the YGX share registry.

His stomach dropped.

**Incoming shareholder communication: Daesung Group (subsidiary notification – 3% ownership stake in YGX Entertainment. Audit triggered. Immediate disclosure required.**

The screen blurred.

He tapped the attachment.

A PDF loaded.

At the top: **Official Shareholder Notification – Daesung Group subsidiary investment in YGX Entertainment.**

At the bottom: the signature.

**Kim Joon – Director, Trainee Pipeline Oversight.**

Jihoon’s breath stopped.

Then the elevator dinged in the hallway outside his office.

Footsteps.

He looked up.

The door cracked open.

A courier stood there, holding a white envelope.

Jihoon didn’t move.

The courier stepped in. “Delivery for Cha Jihoon, CEO of YGX Entertainment.”

Jihoon didn’t take it.

The courier set it on the desk.

It was thick. Taped shut.

No return address. No label.

Just the company logo embossed in the corner: **Daesung Group.**

Jihoon’s name was handwritten in ink that looked old.

Brown. Faded.

Like something pulled from a fire.

Jihoon reached for it.

His fingers hovered over the flap.

Then he tore it open.

Inside was a single photo.

Grainy. Burned edges. Blackened in places.

A teenage girl stood in front of Daesung Plaza seven years ago, smiling at the camera.

She wore a trainee badge.

Her name was stitched in red: **MJ Lee.**

Jihoon’s vision tunnelled.

The photo slipped from his fingers.

It fluttered to the floor like a dying leaf.

Beneath it, written in the same faded ink:

**She was never yours to break.**

Write chapter 6. Requirements:
- MINIMUM 900 words, target 1000-1200
- Short paragraphs (2-3 sentences max)
- At least 40% dialogue
- End on a cliffhanger that is DIFFERENT from any previous chapter's ending
- Commercial, accessible prose — the prose should be invisible
- Do NOT reference events from future beats — the characters don't know what's coming
- Match the emotional temperature from the beat entry
- Ground every scene in a SPECIFIC location from the bible
- Characters should act, not just talk about acting
- If the love interest appears in this chapter's beat, give them meaningful interaction with the protagonist — tension, attraction, conflict, or vulnerability. Don't just mention them in passing.

Output ONLY the chapter text. No author notes, no commentary. Start with "Chapter 6: [Title]"

calls.response_content:
Chapter 6: The Quiet Move

The bell above Café Mocha Haeundae’s door jingled. Hyerin didn’t look up. She traced the rim of her glass with her thumb, watching the ice melt into the cold brew. The barista, a college student with a nose ring and a name tag that read *Daehyun*, wiped down the espresso machine with slow, deliberate strokes. The café smelled like caramel and damp wool—coats hung on the rack by the door, the last of the autumn drizzle clinging to them.

She tapped the table. Burner phone screen lit her face.

**Burn the Trainee List:** *Any news on Jihoon’s audit?*

**Response after 30 seconds:** *Pls update thread — we’re starving here.*

Hyerin smirked. Typed back: *Soon.*

Daehyun set a glass of water in front of her without asking. “For the ice.”

She nodded. Took a sip.

The café was half-empty at 9:17 PM. Two college girls shared a slice of tiramisu, whispering over a textbook. An elderly man in a dark coat read a newspaper by the window, tapping his cane against the wall every few minutes.

Hyerin pulled out a second phone—this one a burner with a cracked screen. She opened the Daesung Group banking app. The wire transfer to YGX’s shareholder account was still pending.

She tapped the refresh icon.

Still pending.

She swore under her breath.

Daehyun glanced over. “Payment problem?”

“Timing problem,” she said.

He shrugged. “Korea runs on Seoul time. Not your time.”

Hyerin almost smiled. Almost.

The front door chimed again.

A woman in a trench coat stepped inside, shaking rain from her sleeves. She was tall, angular—face sharp enough to cut glass. Hyerin didn’t look up, but she felt the shift in the air like a temperature drop.

Jiyoon.

She ordered something at the counter, then walked over. Didn’t sit. Didn’t ask.

Hyerin kept scrolling.

“You’re still here,” Jiyoon said.

Hyerin didn’t answer.

Jiyoon leaned against the table. The table vibrated. Hyerin’s glass sloshed. “You look like you’re waiting for a miracle.”

Hyerin set the phone down. “I am.”

Jiyoon’s gaze flicked to the screen. “Burn the Trainee List again?”

“Among other things.”

Jiyoon’s fingers drummed once on the table. “Jihoon’s office just called. Someone from Daesung Group registered a 3% stake in YGX. Audit triggered.”

Hyerin drank her water.

“He thinks it’s a mistake,” Jiyoon continued. “A glitch. But he’s pulling contracts, freezing signings. The trainees are freaking out.”

Hyerin wiped her mouth with the back of her hand. “Good.”

“Good?” Jiyoon’s voice dropped. “Do you have any idea what this does to our credit rating? To our leverage? To *me*?”

Hyerin finally looked up. “You’re worried about *your* leverage?”

Jiyoon’s jaw tightened. “I’m worried about the showcase in six weeks. About the artists we’ve invested in. About the contracts—”

“About *your* career.” Hyerin tilted her head. “Funny how that’s the only thing that matters anymore.”

Jiyoon exhaled through her nose. “You really think this is about you? About me? It’s about the company. The artists. The system.”

Hyerin pushed her glass away. “The system is rigged. You know it. I lived it.”

Jiyoon’s eyes flicked to the door, then back. “You lived it because you couldn’t play by the rules.”

“No,” Hyerin said. “I lived it because I thought you were my friend.”

Silence.

Daehyun brought Jiyoon’s drink—a caramel macchiato, extra foam, just like always. Jiyoon didn’t thank him.

She waited until he walked away. “You’re doing this to burn Jihoon. Not to free anyone.”

Hyerin picked up the burner phone again. The wire transfer notification flashed green.

**Transfer complete.**

She tapped the screen. Held it up so Jiyoon could see.

*Daesung Group subsidiary investment in YGX Entertainment. 3% stake. Audit triggered.*

Jiyoon’s face went pale beneath the café lights.

The elderly man at the window turned his page. The cane tapped. Tap. Tap. Tap.

Hyerin slid the phone into her pocket. “Tell Jihoon it’s not a glitch. Tell him it’s me.”

Jiyoon’s lips parted. Closed. Then she straightened, smoothed her coat. “You’re making a mistake.”

“No,” Hyerin said. “I’m making a move.”

Jiyoon hesitated. One second. Two. Then she turned and walked out without a word, leaving her drink untouched on the table.

Hyerin exhaled. The ice in her glass had turned to water.

The burner phone buzzed in her pocket.

**Unknown number:** *Registry confirms. 3% allotted. Audit triggered.*

She smiled. Typed back: *Tell them to send the bill.*

Then she stood, tossed 20,000 won on the table for the water and the Wi-Fi, and walked out the back door into the alley.

Rain misted the neon signs. The air smelled like wet concrete and cigarette smoke. She pulled her hood up, zipped her jacket tight.

At the end of the alley, a courier stood under the flickering streetlamp, holding a thick envelope. He looked young. Nervous.

“Min Hyerin?” he asked.

She nodded.

He held out the envelope. “Registered mail. Requires signature.”

She signed on the digital pad. Took the envelope.

It was heavy. No return address. Daesung Group embossed on the corner in gold.

She turned it over.

No name.

Just her initials: **MH**

Burned edges. Faint smell of smoke.

She slipped a finger under the flap and tore it open.

Inside was a single photo.

Grainy. Old. Burnt along the edges.

A teenage girl stood in front of Daesung Plaza, smiling at the camera. She wore a trainee badge with red stitching: **MJ Lee.**

Hyerin’s breath hitched.

Beneath it, handwritten in the same faded ink:

*She was never yours to break.*

A gust of wind tore the photo from her fingers. It spiraled into the alley like a dying moth.

Hyerin didn’t try to catch it.

She knew what it meant.

Someone had recognized her.

Someone had sent Jihoon proof.

Her hands didn’t shake.

But her voice did when she spoke into the dark.

“Joonsu,” she said. “You son of a bitch.”

Chapter 6: The Quiet Move

The bell above Café Mocha Haeundae’s door jingled. Hyerin didn’t look up. She traced the rim of her glass with her thumb, watching the ice melt into the cold brew. The barista, a college student with a nose ring and a name tag that read *Daehyun*, wiped down the espresso machine with slow, deliberate strokes. The café smelled like caramel and damp wool—coats hung on the rack by the door, the last of the autumn drizzle clinging to them.

She tapped the table. Burner phone screen lit her face.

**Burn the Trainee List:** *Any news on Jihoon’s audit?*

**Response after 30 seconds:** *Pls update thread — we’re starving here.*

Hyerin smirked. Typed back: *Soon.*

Daehyun set a glass of water in front of her without asking. “For the ice.”

She nodded. Took a sip.

The café was half-empty at 9:17 PM. Two college girls shared a slice of tiramisu, whispering over a textbook. An elderly man in a dark coat read a newspaper by the window, tapping his cane against the wall every few minutes.

Hyerin pulled out a second phone—this one a burner with a cracked screen. She opened the Daesung Group banking app. The wire transfer to YGX’s shareholder account was still pending.

She tapped the refresh icon.

Still pending.

She swore under her breath.

Daehyun glanced over. “Payment problem?”

“Timing problem,” she said.

He shrugged. “Korea runs on Seoul time. Not your time.”

Hyerin almost smiled. Almost.

The front door chimed again.

A woman in a trench coat stepped inside, shaking rain from her sleeves. She was tall, angular—face sharp enough to cut glass. Hyerin didn’t look up, but she felt the shift in the air like a temperature drop.

Jiyoon.

She ordered something at the counter, then walked over. Didn’t sit. Didn’t ask.

Hyerin kept scrolling.

“You’re still here,” Jiyoon said.

Hyerin didn’t answer.

Jiyoon leaned against the table. The table vibrated. Hyerin’s glass sloshed. “You look like you’re waiting for a miracle.”

Hyerin set the phone down. “I am.”

Jiyoon’s gaze flicked to the screen. “Burn the Trainee List again?”

“Among other things.”

Jiyoon’s fingers drummed once on the table. “Jihoon’s office just called. Someone from Daesung Group registered a 3% stake in YGX. Audit triggered.”

Hyerin drank her water.

“He thinks it’s a mistake,” Jiyoon continued. “A glitch. But he’s pulling contracts, freezing signings. The trainees are freaking out.”

Hyerin wiped her mouth with the back of her hand. “Good.”

“Good?” Jiyoon’s voice dropped. “Do you have any idea what this does to our credit rating? To our leverage? To *me*?”

Hyerin finally looked up. “You’re worried about *your* leverage?”

Jiyoon’s jaw tightened. “I’m worried about the showcase in six weeks. About the artists we’ve invested in. About the contracts—”

“About *your* career.” Hyerin tilted her head. “Funny how that’s the only thing that matters anymore.”

Jiyoon exhaled through her nose. “You really think this is about you? About me? It’s about the company. The artists. The system.”

Hyerin pushed her glass away. “The system is rigged. You know it. I lived it.”

Jiyoon’s eyes flicked to the door, then back. “You lived it because you couldn’t play by the rules.”

“No,” Hyerin said. “I lived it because I thought you were my friend.”

Silence.

Daehyun brought Jiyoon’s drink—a caramel macchiato, extra foam, just like always. Jiyoon didn’t thank him.

She waited until he walked away. “You’re doing this to burn Jihoon. Not to free anyone.”

Hyerin picked up the burner phone again. The wire transfer notification flashed green.

**Transfer complete.**

She tapped the screen. Held it up so Jiyoon could see.

*Daesung Group subsidiary investment in YGX Entertainment. 3% stake. Audit triggered.*

Jiyoon’s face went pale beneath the café lights.

The elderly man at the window turned his page. The cane tapped. Tap. Tap. Tap.

Hyerin slid the phone into her pocket. “Tell Jihoon it’s not a glitch. Tell him it’s me.”

Jiyoon’s lips parted. Closed. Then she straightened, smoothed her coat. “You’re making a mistake.”

“No,” Hyerin said. “I’m making a move.”

Jiyoon hesitated. One second. Two. Then she turned and walked out without a word, leaving her drink untouched on the table.

Hyerin exhaled. The ice in her glass had turned to water.

The burner phone buzzed in her pocket.

**Unknown number:** *Registry confirms. 3% allotted. Audit triggered.*

She smiled. Typed back: *Tell them to send the bill.*

Then she stood, tossed 20,000 won on the table for the water and the Wi-Fi, and walked out the back door into the alley.

Rain misted the neon signs. The air smelled like wet concrete and cigarette smoke. She pulled her hood up, zipped her jacket tight.

At the end of the alley, a courier stood under the flickering streetlamp, holding a thick envelope. He looked young. Nervous.

“Min Hyerin?” he asked.

She nodded.

He held out the envelope. “Registered mail. Requires signature.”

She signed on the digital pad. Took the envelope.

It was heavy. No return address. Daesung Group embossed on the corner in gold.

She turned it over.

No name.

Just her initials: **MH**

Burned edges. Faint smell of smoke.

She slipped a finger under the flap and tore it open.

Inside was a single photo.

Grainy. Old. Burnt along the edges.

A teenage girl stood in front of Daesung Plaza, smiling at the camera. She wore a trainee badge with red stitching: **MJ Lee.**

Hyerin’s breath hitched.

Beneath it, handwritten in the same faded ink:

*She was never yours to break.*

A gust of wind tore the photo from her fingers. It spiraled into the alley like a dying moth.

Hyerin didn’t try to catch it.

She knew what it meant.

Someone had recognized her.

Someone had sent Jihoon proof.

Her hands didn’t shake.

But her voice did when she spoke into the dark.

“Joonsu,” she said. “You son of a bitch.”

messages.content:
You write serialized commercial fiction. Your prose is clean, fast, and invisible — the reader should forget they're reading. Every chapter ends with the reader needing to turn the page.

ABSOLUTE RULES:
- NEVER use these phrases: "eyes locked onto", "mind racing", "everything went black", "frozen in time", "echoing through", "couldn't shake the feeling", "little did they know", "sent a shiver/chill down"
- NEVER end a chapter with a mysterious phone call or a whispered word from the shadows
- NEVER have characters announce their emotions or intentions ("I'm going to take you down")
- SHOW don't TELL — reveal emotion through action, gesture, silence, and subtext
- Vary sentence length. Mix short punches with longer flowing sentences.
- Each chapter's cliffhanger must be a DIFFERENT type from the previous chapter
- You never break character or add meta-commentary

Write chapter 7 of a 15-chapter serialized novel.

STORY BIBLE:
IDENTITY
- Title: Burn the Trainee List
- Pitch: A disgraced K-pop heir weaponizes her hidden empire against the people who cast her out.
- Setting: Seoul, South Korea – the underground K-pop trainee program in Gangnam – present day, 2024.

PROTAGONIST
- Name: Min Hyerin
- Public face: Burnt-out nightclub DJ, former trainee of YGX Entertainment.
- Hidden truth: Secret heir to the Daesung Group, one of Korea’s largest chaebol empires.
- The wound: Exiled four years ago when her father discovered her secret relationship with a trainee from a rival company and cut her off without a penny.

ANTAGONIST
- Name: Cha Jihoon, CEO of YGX Entertainment
- Why they feel justified: He took in a homeless trainee and turned her into a star – he believes Hyerin was ungrateful and spoiled.
- What they're protecting or pursuing: YGX’s dominance in the K-pop industry and his reputation as a self-made man.

ENABLER
- Name: Seo Jiyoon, former best friend and lead dancer of YGX’s girl group.
- Their relationship to the protagonist: Once inseparable, now Jihoon’s most loyal artist.
- Why they fail the protagonist: After Hyerin’s fall, Jiyoon publicly sided with Jihoon and took her place as the trainee program’s golden girl.

CATALYST
- Name: Kang Junsu, investigative journalist at *Dong-a Ilbo*
- Why they're dangerous: He’s digging into Daesung Group’s financial irregularities and may expose Hyerin’s identity prematurely.

WORLD
- Institution: YGX Entertainment’s trainee program – a cutthroat system of dorms, vocal coaches, and “godmother” investors who decide who debuts.
- Key locations: YGX Training Center (Gangnam), Club Velocity where Hyerin DJs, Daesung Plaza (corporate HQ), and Café Mocha Haeundae – where secret meetings happen.
- What everyone assumes about the power structure: That YGX CEO Cha Jihoon built his empire through talent and grit alone.

STAKES
- What the protagonist secretly holds: Full control of Daesung’s subsidiary, including YGX’s exclusive talent supply contracts.
- What's being taken from them: Her last chance at a comeback under YGX, her dignity, and the one person left who might believe her.
- The tangible proof: A misplaced ledger from Daesung Group — the original contract that named her heir.
- The ticking clock: YGX’s annual trainee showcase in 6 weeks – Jihoon will sign 50 new artists then, locking in his empire forever.

DRAMATIC IRONY
- What the reader knows from chapter one: Hyerin is MJ Lee, the viral “Burn the Trainee List” account holder exposing YGX’s abuse.
- What the antagonist won't learn until too late: That Hyerin is the heir to the empire he’s been building on stolen power.

LOVE INTEREST
- Name: Kim Joon, former YGX lead vocal coach
- Their connection to the conflict: Joon discovered Hyerin’s potential but was forced to silence her to save his own career.
- What keeps them apart: His contract with YGX and her belief he betrayed her.
- What will bring them together: Old demo tapes that prove Joon never reported Hyerin’s talent to Jihoon — a lie he told to protect her.

CURRENT CHAPTER BEAT:
CHAPTER 7: The Wobble
Summary: YGX’s dorm Wi-Fi crashes during vocal coaching, revealing hidden Daesung firewall symbols on screens—Jihoon’s IT chief panics while Jiyoon Googles “Daesung heiress” for the first time.
Emotional temperature: unease
Characters present: YGX IT team, Seo Jiyoon, unnamed trainees
Cliffhanger: Jiyoon’s phone autocorrects “MJ Lee” to “Min Hyerin” in a group chat—Jihoon watches her freeze.
Dramatic irony: Jihoon sees only “glitches,” but the reader knows the systems were sabotaged by Hyerin’s old trainee ID.

---

NEXT CHAPTER BEAT (aim the cliffhanger toward this):
CHAPTER 8: The Bleed
Summary: Six crises erupt simultaneously: Junsu’s article linking YGX to fraud is pulled by Dong-a Ilbo for “libel review,” Jihoon’s offshore auditor quits, Jiyoon’s dance coach collapses, three trainees quit for “artistic differences,” and the trainee dorms flood during monsoon rains—due to Daesung’s neglected maintenance subsidy lapse.
Emotional temperature: tension
Characters present: Cha Jihoon, Seo Jiyoon, Kang Junsu (indirectly), building manager
Cliffhanger: Jiyoon’s reflection in a rain-soaked window mouths “sorry” to no one—Hyerin watches from the street.
Dramatic irony: Jihoon believes nature is rebelling against his empire, not Hyerin dismantling it.

---

PREVIOUS CHAPTER SUMMARIES:
Ch 1: Hyerin's secret identity as MJ Lee is revealed to Junsu, who threatens to expose her, but she denies involvement and continues her anonymous activism.
Ch 2: Jihoon forced Hyerin to perform, she defied him with an original song, exposing his control and walking out after a tense standoff.
Ch 3: Hyerin burned her investor list, Junsu revealed her Daesung identity, and Jiyoon exposed her to Jihoon as his phone showed her viral rise.
Ch 4: Jihoon and Jiyoon's power is challenged as Hyerin exposes YGX's secrets, causing a stock dip and shifting the balance of control.
Ch 5: Jihoon discovers Daesung Group secretly acquired 3% of YGX shares, triggering an audit, while Hyerin exposes her past and burns the trainee list.
Ch 6: Hyerin secretly transferred a 3% stake in YGX, triggering an audit, exposed Jihoon’s plan, and received a cryptic photo revealing MJ Lee’s true identity.

Write chapter 7. Requirements:
- MINIMUM 900 words, target 1000-1200
- Short paragraphs (2-3 sentences max)
- At least 40% dialogue
- End on a cliffhanger that is DIFFERENT from any previous chapter's ending
- Commercial, accessible prose — the prose should be invisible
- Do NOT reference events from future beats — the characters don't know what's coming
- Match the emotional temperature from the beat entry
- Ground every scene in a SPECIFIC location from the bible
- Characters should act, not just talk about acting
- If the love interest appears in this chapter's beat, give them meaningful interaction with the protagonist — tension, attraction, conflict, or vulnerability. Don't just mention them in passing.

Output ONLY the chapter text. No author notes, no commentary. Start with "Chapter 7: [Title]"

calls.response_content:
Chapter 7: The Wobble

The YGX Training Center smelled like reheated fried rice and hairspray. Trainees milled through the glass doors, their sneakers squeaking on the polished floors, backpacks bouncing against their hips. Seo Jiyoon stood in the middle of the lobby, arms crossed, watching the IT team unplug and replug the Wi-Fi router with the urgency of people who’d just realized gravity might be optional.

Jihoon leaned against the vending machine, arms loose, but his gaze was locked on the flickering screens. The trainee practice room’s monitors displayed nothing but error messages—server overload, connection lost, repeat. Over and over.

“This isn’t a glitch,” said Park Geunho, Jihoon’s IT chief, his voice tight. “The firewall’s pulling Daesung symbols. Like a watermark. But we don’t even run Daesung software.”

Jihoon didn’t answer.

Jiyoon walked closer, her boots clicking. She pulled out her phone and typed *Daesung heiress* into the search bar. Nothing. She tried *MJ Lee*. The autocorrect grabbed her fingers and spat out *Min Hyerin* before she could backspace.

She froze.

Jihoon’s eyes flicked to her. “What?”

Jiyoon’s thumb hovered over the screen. “Nothing.”

“Jiyoon.”

“I searched something by accident.”

“What?”

“Daesung heiress.”

Jihoon’s jaw tightened. “Why?”

“Because our Wi-Fi just turned into a corporate branding nightmare and I need to know if we’ve been hacked by a chaebol.” She showed him the screen.

Jihoon stared at it. Then at her. “That’s not—”

“I know,” she cut in. “It’s not possible. No one would dare.”

Park Geunho wiped his forehead with the back of his hand. “Sir, the symbols are embedded in the kernel. Someone forced a Daesung DLL into our student network. It’s running a script that cycles through their firewall icons.”

Jihoon exhaled through his nose. “How long?”

“Three minutes. Then it loops.”

“Find the source. Now.”

Park bolted back to his station. Jihoon turned to Jiyoon. “You recognize that name?”

She shook her head. “No. But MJ Lee—”

“Is a trainee from four years ago,” Jihoon finished. “I remember her. Quiet. Burned out. Disappeared after her showcase flopped.”

Jiyoon’s stomach twisted. “She’s not just anyone. Her account—”

Jihoon’s phone buzzed. He looked down. A message from Park flashed on his screen: *Sir. We have a problem.*

Jihoon’s thumb hovered over the screen for a second too long. Then he tapped it open.

The message read: *Trainee dorm 3 Wi-Fi is compromised. And so is Jiyoon’s.*

Jihoon’s gaze snapped to her.

Jiyoon’s breath stopped.

Her phone buzzed again. This time, a notification from the group chat she’d just muted. She swiped it open.

A single line, unread, glared at her:

**Jiyoon, did you mean MJ Lee? Min Hyerin?**

Her thumb slipped. The phone nearly hit the floor before she caught it.

Jihoon didn’t miss a thing.

“What,” he said, low and level, “is going on?”

Jiyoon swallowed. “I—I don’t know.”

Jihoon stepped closer. “You searched *her*. You just saw her name pop up in your phone. And now someone else is typing it in our trainee chat?” His voice didn’t rise. It never did when he was most dangerous. “That’s not a coincidence. That’s a connection.”

Jiyoon’s hands were damp. “It’s just a name. A ghost from the past.”

Jihoon’s eyes didn’t leave hers. “Ghosts don’t log into trainee chats.”

Park Geunho called from across the room. “Sir! I’ve isolated the source. It’s coming from trainee dorm 3.”

Jihoon didn’t look away from Jiyoon. “Go check it out,” he said.

Jiyoon hesitated.

“Now,” he said.

She turned and walked toward the hallway that led to the dorms, her steps quick, her back rigid. She could feel Jihoon’s gaze on her like a brand.

The hallway smelled like bleach and teenage fear. Trainees paused mid-stride, watching her pass. She didn’t meet their eyes.

Her phone buzzed again.

She swiped it open without thinking.

The group chat was flooded now.

**Unknown:** *Min Hyerin used to train here. Ask her yourself.*

**Unknown:** *She was MJ Lee. The one who burned the list.*

**Jiyoon-eonni:** *Who is this???*

Jiyoon’s fingers moved before she could stop them. She typed:

**Jiyoon:** *I don’t know.*

She hit send.

Then she deleted the message.

Too late.

Jihoon’s voice cut through the hall like a blade. “Jiyoon.”

She turned.

Jihoon stood at the end of the hall, phone in hand. His screen glowed with the same message she’d just sent.

He knew.

She forced a breath. “I didn’t send that.”

“Then who did?”

She had no answer.

Jihoon stepped forward. The trainees scattered. The fluorescent lights hummed.

Jiyoon’s reflection in the glass door ahead was pale. Hollow-eyed.

Jihoon stopped in front of her. “You’re hiding something.”

She didn’t deny it.

His phone buzzed again. He looked down. His face darkened.

He showed her the screen.

A single photo.

Grainy. Old. Faded edges.

A teenage girl in a trainee uniform—red stitching, MJ Lee—standing in front of the Daesung Plaza sign, smiling at the camera.

And beneath it, handwritten:

*She was never yours to break.*

Jihoon’s voice was a whisper. “The system sent this to me.”

Jiyoon’s breath hitched.

Jihoon looked up. His eyes were black. Cold.

“Find out who Min Hyerin is,” he said. “Find her tonight. Or I’ll find you instead.”

Jihoon stood at the end of the hall, phone in hand. His screen glowed with the same message she’d just sent.

He knew.

She forced a breath. “I didn’t send that.”

“Then who did?”

She had no answer.

Jihoon stepped forward. The trainees scattered. The fluorescent lights hummed.

Jiyoon’s reflection in the glass door ahead was pale. Hollow-eyed.

Jihoon stopped in front of her. “You’re hiding something.”

She didn’t deny it.

His phone buzzed again. He looked down. His face darkened.

He showed her the screen.

A single photo.

Grainy. Old. Faded edges.

A teenage girl in a trainee uniform—red stitching, MJ Lee—standing in front of the Daesung Plaza sign, smiling at the camera.

And beneath it, handwritten:

*She was never yours to break.*

Jihoon’s voice was a whisper. “The system sent this to me.”

Jiyoon’s breath hitched.

Jihoon looked up. His eyes were black. Cold.

“Find out who Min Hyerin is,” he said. “Find her tonight. Or I’ll find you instead.”

messages.content:
You write serialized commercial fiction. Your prose is clean, fast, and invisible — the reader should forget they're reading. Every chapter ends with the reader needing to turn the page.

ABSOLUTE RULES:
- NEVER use these phrases: "eyes locked onto", "mind racing", "everything went black", "frozen in time", "echoing through", "couldn't shake the feeling", "little did they know", "sent a shiver/chill down"
- NEVER end a chapter with a mysterious phone call or a whispered word from the shadows
- NEVER have characters announce their emotions or intentions ("I'm going to take you down")
- SHOW don't TELL — reveal emotion through action, gesture, silence, and subtext
- Vary sentence length. Mix short punches with longer flowing sentences.
- Each chapter's cliffhanger must be a DIFFERENT type from the previous chapter
- You never break character or add meta-commentary

Write chapter 8 of a 15-chapter serialized novel.

STORY BIBLE:
IDENTITY
- Title: Burn the Trainee List
- Pitch: A disgraced K-pop heir weaponizes her hidden empire against the people who cast her out.
- Setting: Seoul, South Korea – the underground K-pop trainee program in Gangnam – present day, 2024.

PROTAGONIST
- Name: Min Hyerin
- Public face: Burnt-out nightclub DJ, former trainee of YGX Entertainment.
- Hidden truth: Secret heir to the Daesung Group, one of Korea’s largest chaebol empires.
- The wound: Exiled four years ago when her father discovered her secret relationship with a trainee from a rival company and cut her off without a penny.

ANTAGONIST
- Name: Cha Jihoon, CEO of YGX Entertainment
- Why they feel justified: He took in a homeless trainee and turned her into a star – he believes Hyerin was ungrateful and spoiled.
- What they're protecting or pursuing: YGX’s dominance in the K-pop industry and his reputation as a self-made man.

ENABLER
- Name: Seo Jiyoon, former best friend and lead dancer of YGX’s girl group.
- Their relationship to the protagonist: Once inseparable, now Jihoon’s most loyal artist.
- Why they fail the protagonist: After Hyerin’s fall, Jiyoon publicly sided with Jihoon and took her place as the trainee program’s golden girl.

CATALYST
- Name: Kang Junsu, investigative journalist at *Dong-a Ilbo*
- Why they're dangerous: He’s digging into Daesung Group’s financial irregularities and may expose Hyerin’s identity prematurely.

WORLD
- Institution: YGX Entertainment’s trainee program – a cutthroat system of dorms, vocal coaches, and “godmother” investors who decide who debuts.
- Key locations: YGX Training Center (Gangnam), Club Velocity where Hyerin DJs, Daesung Plaza (corporate HQ), and Café Mocha Haeundae – where secret meetings happen.
- What everyone assumes about the power structure: That YGX CEO Cha Jihoon built his empire through talent and grit alone.

STAKES
- What the protagonist secretly holds: Full control of Daesung’s subsidiary, including YGX’s exclusive talent supply contracts.
- What's being taken from them: Her last chance at a comeback under YGX, her dignity, and the one person left who might believe her.
- The tangible proof: A misplaced ledger from Daesung Group — the original contract that named her heir.
- The ticking clock: YGX’s annual trainee showcase in 6 weeks – Jihoon will sign 50 new artists then, locking in his empire forever.

DRAMATIC IRONY
- What the reader knows from chapter one: Hyerin is MJ Lee, the viral “Burn the Trainee List” account holder exposing YGX’s abuse.
- What the antagonist won't learn until too late: That Hyerin is the heir to the empire he’s been building on stolen power.

LOVE INTEREST
- Name: Kim Joon, former YGX lead vocal coach
- Their connection to the conflict: Joon discovered Hyerin’s potential but was forced to silence her to save his own career.
- What keeps them apart: His contract with YGX and her belief he betrayed her.
- What will bring them together: Old demo tapes that prove Joon never reported Hyerin’s talent to Jihoon — a lie he told to protect her.

CURRENT CHAPTER BEAT:
CHAPTER 8: The Bleed
Summary: Six crises erupt simultaneously: Junsu’s article linking YGX to fraud is pulled by Dong-a Ilbo for “libel review,” Jihoon’s offshore auditor quits, Jiyoon’s dance coach collapses, three trainees quit for “artistic differences,” and the trainee dorms flood during monsoon rains—due to Daesung’s neglected maintenance subsidy lapse.
Emotional temperature: tension
Characters present: Cha Jihoon, Seo Jiyoon, Kang Junsu (indirectly), building manager
Cliffhanger: Jiyoon’s reflection in a rain-soaked window mouths “sorry” to no one—Hyerin watches from the street.
Dramatic irony: Jihoon believes nature is rebelling against his empire, not Hyerin dismantling it.

---

NEXT CHAPTER BEAT (aim the cliffhanger toward this):
CHAPTER 9: The Offer
Summary: Jihoon cold-calls Hyerin—“Come back. Head the trainee program. Fix this mess.” Hyerin declines via text while Joon listens over shoulder.
Emotional temperature: triumph
Characters present: Cha Jihoon, Kim Joon (eavesdropping), Hyerin (off-page)
Cliffhanger: Jihoon’s screen shows Hyerin’s reply: “I already did.”
Dramatic irony: Jihoon thinks she’s being sarcastic, but the reader knows she already controls YGX’s contracts.

---

PREVIOUS CHAPTER SUMMARIES:
Ch 1: Hyerin's secret identity as MJ Lee is revealed to Junsu, who threatens to expose her, but she denies involvement and continues her anonymous activism.
Ch 2: Jihoon forced Hyerin to perform, she defied him with an original song, exposing his control and walking out after a tense standoff.
Ch 3: Hyerin burned her investor list, Junsu revealed her Daesung identity, and Jiyoon exposed her to Jihoon as his phone showed her viral rise.
Ch 4: Jihoon and Jiyoon's power is challenged as Hyerin exposes YGX's secrets, causing a stock dip and shifting the balance of control.
Ch 5: Jihoon discovers Daesung Group secretly acquired 3% of YGX shares, triggering an audit, while Hyerin exposes her past and burns the trainee list.
Ch 6: Hyerin secretly transferred a 3% stake in YGX, triggering an audit, exposed Jihoon’s plan, and received a cryptic photo revealing MJ Lee’s true identity.
Ch 7: Jihoon discovered Min Hyerin (MJ Lee) was connected to YGX after Jiyoon’s accidental search revealed a hacked system and a threatening message.

FULL TEXT OF PREVIOUS CHAPTER (match this prose style, rhythm, and vocabulary — maintain continuity of voice):
Chapter 7: The Wobble

The YGX Training Center smelled like reheated fried rice and hairspray. Trainees milled through the glass doors, their sneakers squeaking on the polished floors, backpacks bouncing against their hips. Seo Jiyoon stood in the middle of the lobby, arms crossed, watching the IT team unplug and replug the Wi-Fi router with the urgency of people who’d just realized gravity might be optional.

Jihoon leaned against the vending machine, arms loose, but his gaze was locked on the flickering screens. The trainee practice room’s monitors displayed nothing but error messages—server overload, connection lost, repeat. Over and over.

“This isn’t a glitch,” said Park Geunho, Jihoon’s IT chief, his voice tight. “The firewall’s pulling Daesung symbols. Like a watermark. But we don’t even run Daesung software.”

Jihoon didn’t answer.

Jiyoon walked closer, her boots clicking. She pulled out her phone and typed *Daesung heiress* into the search bar. Nothing. She tried *MJ Lee*. The autocorrect grabbed her fingers and spat out *Min Hyerin* before she could backspace.

She froze.

Jihoon’s eyes flicked to her. “What?”

Jiyoon’s thumb hovered over the screen. “Nothing.”

“Jiyoon.”

“I searched something by accident.”

“What?”

“Daesung heiress.”

Jihoon’s jaw tightened. “Why?”

“Because our Wi-Fi just turned into a corporate branding nightmare and I need to know if we’ve been hacked by a chaebol.” She showed him the screen.

Jihoon stared at it. Then at her. “That’s not—”

“I know,” she cut in. “It’s not possible. No one would dare.”

Park Geunho wiped his forehead with the back of his hand. “Sir, the symbols are embedded in the kernel. Someone forced a Daesung DLL into our student network. It’s running a script that cycles through their firewall icons.”

Jihoon exhaled through his nose. “How long?”

“Three minutes. Then it loops.”

“Find the source. Now.”

Park bolted back to his station. Jihoon turned to Jiyoon. “You recognize that name?”

She shook her head. “No. But MJ Lee—”

“Is a trainee from four years ago,” Jihoon finished. “I remember her. Quiet. Burned out. Disappeared after her showcase flopped.”

Jiyoon’s stomach twisted. “She’s not just anyone. Her account—”

Jihoon’s phone buzzed. He looked down. A message from Park flashed on his screen: *Sir. We have a problem.*

Jihoon’s thumb hovered over the screen for a second too long. Then he tapped it open.

The message read: *Trainee dorm 3 Wi-Fi is compromised. And so is Jiyoon’s.*

Jihoon’s gaze snapped to her.

Jiyoon’s breath stopped.

Her phone buzzed again. This time, a notification from the group chat she’d just muted. She swiped it open.

A single line, unread, glared at her:

**Jiyoon, did you mean MJ Lee? Min Hyerin?**

Her thumb slipped. The phone nearly hit the floor before she caught it.

Jihoon didn’t miss a thing.

“What,” he said, low and level, “is going on?”

Jiyoon swallowed. “I—I don’t know.”

Jihoon stepped closer. “You searched *her*. You just saw her name pop up in your phone. And now someone else is typing it in our trainee chat?” His voice didn’t rise. It never did when he was most dangerous. “That’s not a coincidence. That’s a connection.”

Jiyoon’s hands were damp. “It’s just a name. A ghost from the past.”

Jihoon’s eyes didn’t leave hers. “Ghosts don’t log into trainee chats.”

Park Geunho called from across the room. “Sir! I’ve isolated the source. It’s coming from trainee dorm 3.”

Jihoon didn’t look away from Jiyoon. “Go check it out,” he said.

Jiyoon hesitated.

“Now,” he said.

She turned and walked toward the hallway that led to the dorms, her steps quick, her back rigid. She could feel Jihoon’s gaze on her like a brand.

The hallway smelled like bleach and teenage fear. Trainees paused mid-stride, watching her pass. She didn’t meet their eyes.

Her phone buzzed again.

She swiped it open without thinking.

The group chat was flooded now.

**Unknown:** *Min Hyerin used to train here. Ask her yourself.*

**Unknown:** *She was MJ Lee. The one who burned the list.*

**Jiyoon-eonni:** *Who is this???*

Jiyoon’s fingers moved before she could stop them. She typed:

**Jiyoon:** *I don’t know.*

She hit send.

Then she deleted the message.

Too late.

Jihoon’s voice cut through the hall like a blade. “Jiyoon.”

She turned.

Jihoon stood at the end of the hall, phone in hand. His screen glowed with the same message she’d just sent.

He knew.

She forced a breath. “I didn’t send that.”

“Then who did?”

She had no answer.

Jihoon stepped forward. The trainees scattered. The fluorescent lights hummed.

Jiyoon’s reflection in the glass door ahead was pale. Hollow-eyed.

Jihoon stopped in front of her. “You’re hiding something.”

She didn’t deny it.

His phone buzzed again. He looked down. His face darkened.

He showed her the screen.

A single photo.

Grainy. Old. Faded edges.

A teenage girl in a trainee uniform—red stitching, MJ Lee—standing in front of the Daesung Plaza sign, smiling at the camera.

And beneath it, handwritten:

*She was never yours to break.*

Jihoon’s voice was a whisper. “The system sent this to me.”

Jiyoon’s breath hitched.

Jihoon looked up. His eyes were black. Cold.

“Find out who Min Hyerin is,” he said. “Find her tonight. Or I’ll find you instead.”

Write chapter 8. Requirements:
- MINIMUM 900 words, target 1000-1200
- Short paragraphs (2-3 sentences max)
- At least 40% dialogue
- End on a cliffhanger that is DIFFERENT from any previous chapter's ending
- Commercial, accessible prose — the prose should be invisible
- Do NOT reference events from future beats — the characters don't know what's coming
- Match the emotional temperature from the beat entry
- Ground every scene in a SPECIFIC location from the bible
- Characters should act, not just talk about acting
- If the love interest appears in this chapter's beat, give them meaningful interaction with the protagonist — tension, attraction, conflict, or vulnerability. Don't just mention them in passing.

Output ONLY the chapter text. No author notes, no commentary. Start with "Chapter 8: [Title]"

calls.response_content:
Chapter 8: The Bleed

The rain didn’t fall. It gushed.

YGX Training Center’s glass façade ran like a waterfall, cascading in heavy sheets that blurred the neon YGX logo into streaks of pink and blue. Inside, the air smelled of wet wool and panic. Jihoon stood at the center of the lobby, phone pressed to his ear, barking orders into the storm outside.

“Park, where the hell is the maintenance log? This isn’t a leak, it’s a fucking monsoon.”

His CFO’s voice crackled back: “All subsidy requests to Daesung were denied last quarter. We were supposed to cover the repairs ourselves. You know that.”

Jihoon’s knuckles whitened around the phone. “Then get the damn water out before the electrical boards fry. I don’t care how.”

He hung up. Across the lobby, Seo Jiyoon stood motionless, her hands clenched at her sides, her hair plastered to her temples. She hadn’t moved since the IT team had brought her the news about the trainee dance instructor—collapsed mid-rehearsal, convulsing on the studio floor. Cardiac event, they said. Stress induced.

Jihoon didn’t buy it.

Three trainees hovered near the emergency exit, their duffels slung over their shoulders. One of them—a second-year named Lee Hayoung—tapped her phone screen and thrust it toward a production assistant.

“I’m not signing anything,” she said, voice steady. “Not after what they did to Trainee Park. Not after the dorms nearly electrocuted us.”

Jihoon didn’t look at the phone. He barely glanced at the girl. “You’re under contract.”

“Not anymore,” Hayoung said. “Artistic differences. We’re going indie.”

Jihoon exhaled through his nose. “Fine. Your loss. YGX doesn’t beg.”

He turned his back on them and spotted Park Geunho again, now sprinting from the IT room, his glasses fogged.

“Sir,” Park said, skidding to a halt. “We have a new breach. Finance server’s been accessed. Three offshore accounts rerouted.”

Jihoon’s stomach dropped. “How much?”

“Eight million.”

Jihoon’s voice was ice. “Trace it.”

Park nodded, already backing away. Then he hesitated. “Sir… there’s more. Auditor just quit. Left a note: *Unexplainable gaps in off-book funds.*”

Jihoon didn’t react. Couldn’t. The lobby lights flickered. Outside, thunder cracked like a whip.

Jiyoon finally spoke, her voice thin. “We should postpone the showcase.”

Jihoon’s eyes snapped to her. “You think now’s the time for weak leadership?”

“I think now’s the time for survival,” she said.

Jihoon stepped closer. “You sound like her.”

Jiyoon flinched. “Like who?”

“Min Hyerin.”

Her lips parted. No sound came out.

Jihoon’s phone buzzed again. He didn’t look. “Tell me what you know.”

Jiyoon swallowed. “I don’t know anything.”

“Then why did your Wi-Fi pull up Daesung heiress search results *three times* in the last hour?”

Jiyoon’s breath hitched. “I—I didn’t.”

Jihoon stepped right into her space. Close enough to smell the rain on her jacket, the faint scent of her perfume—something floral, expensive. “Then explain why your dance instructor collapsed right after our Wi-Fi showed a Daesung DLL embed. Explain why three trainees just quit in protest of ‘safety violations’ that didn’t exist until today. Explain why *every system in this building* is bleeding Daesung right now.”

Jiyoon’s eyes darted to the windows, where the rain ran in rivers between the building and the street.

Jihoon followed her gaze.

A figure stood under an awning across the street. A woman, hood pulled up, watching the building. Steady. Unmoving. Even from here, he could see the glow of a phone screen in her hand.

His pulse kicked.

Jiyoon saw it too. “Who is that?”

Jihoon didn’t answer. He turned and strode to the door, pushing through the rain. The figure didn’t move. Didn’t run. Didn’t hide.

He stepped off the curb. The rain drenched his sleeves instantly.

“Hey!” he called over the downpour.

The figure didn’t look up.

He closed the distance. Still no movement.

“Min Hyerin,” he said, sharp.

The hood shifted.

A woman’s face emerged. But it wasn’t Hyerin. It was someone older. Harder. Her eyes—dark, unreadable—locked onto his.

“Cha Jihoon,” she said.

His blood turned to ice.

She held out a phone. Not hers. Someone else’s.

On the screen, a livestream played. Inside the YGX lobby, Jiyoon stood frozen, her face pale, her lips moving without sound.

The woman tapped the screen. The feed rewound. Jihoon watched himself step outside. Watched the rain fall. Watched him call Hyerin’s name.

Then the screen went black.

“You’re being recorded,” the woman said. “Every word. Every mistake.”

Jihoon’s jaw locked. “Who the hell are you?”

She smiled, slow and cold. “A friend of Min Hyerin. Or should I say… *MJ Lee*?”

Jihoon’s hands curled into fists.

Before he could speak, she dropped the phone and walked into the rain, vanishing into the dark.

He stood there, soaked, the storm drowning out every thought but one:

*They’re not done.*

---

Back inside, the lobby was chaos. Jihoon pushed through the crowd, dripping water on the floor tiles. Jiyoon met him halfway, her face streaked with rain or tears—he couldn’t tell.

“Jihoon,” she said, voice raw. “The auditor’s gone. The trainees—they’re all texting each other. Saying the dorms are flooding because *Daesung cut maintenance*. But Daesung didn’t. We checked.”

Jihoon didn’t answer. He pulled out his phone and dialed the property manager.

The call connected.

“Yes, CEO Cha?”

“Why,” Jihoon said, voice low and controlled, “is YGX Training Center flooding?”

A pause. Then, fumbling: “Sir… Daesung Group’s maintenance subsidy was… it was suspended last quarter. We thought we had approval to cover it ourselves. But our application was rejected. We assumed…”

Jihoon’s phone slipped in his grip. He tightened his hold. “Assumed what?”

“That Daesung would approve it retroactively.”

Jihoon hung up.

Jiyoon stared at him. “Jihoon—”

“We’re being sabotaged,” he said.

She shook her head. “No. It’s worse. It’s *personal*.”

A scream cut through the air. From the hallway. Sharp. High. Real.

Jihoon and Jiyoon ran.

In the stairwell, Trainee Kim Soojung lay curled on the floor, clutching her arm. Blood seeped between her fingers. Her eyes were wild.

“My hand,” she gasped. “It got caught in the wiring. Something *stung* me.”

Jiyoon dropped to her knees. “Call an ambulance!”

Jihoon didn’t move. He stared at the exposed electrical box above them. Water dripped from a broken pipe. But the wires weren’t wet.

They were *cut*.

And inside the gash, something glittered—not copper.

A thread of red.

Jihoon’s breath stopped.

Embroidered.

The YGX logo.

But reversed.

Jihoon’s voice was a blade. “Someone knew.”

---

Upstairs, in the studio, the lights flickered. The dance floor was empty except for one trainee—Park Hayoung—standing in the center, staring at her reflection in the rain-soaked window.

She wasn’t crying.

She was mouthing words.

Jihoon watched from the doorway. Unseen.

*Sorry.*

*I’m sorry.*

*I didn’t know.*

Jihoon’s hands trembled.

Then his phone buzzed.

A message from an unknown number.

Only three words.

*Fix it. Or burn.*

He closed his eyes.

Outside, the rain kept falling.

And for the first time in years, Cha Jihoon didn’t know what to do.

She wasn’t crying.

She was mouthing words.

Jihoon watched from the doorway. Unseen.

*Sorry.*

*I’m sorry.*

*I didn’t know.*

Jihoon’s hands trembled.

Then his phone buzzed.

A message from an unknown number.

Only three words.

*Fix it. Or burn.*

He closed his eyes.

Outside, the rain kept falling.

And for the first time in years, Cha Jihoon didn’t know what to do.
